infobox.value: | * [v](/wiki/Template:History_of_baseball "Template:History of baseball") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:History_of_baseball "Template talk:History of baseball") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:History_of_baseball "Special:EditPage/Template:History of baseball")

is a [bat-and-ball](/wiki/Bat-and-ball_games "Bat-and-ball games") [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") played between two [teams](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") of nine players each, taking turns [batting](/wiki/Batting_(baseball) "Batting (baseball)") and [fielding](/wiki/Fielding_(baseball) "Fielding (baseball)"). The game occurs over the course of several [plays](/wiki/Pitch_(baseball) "Pitch (baseball)"), with each play beginning when a player on the [fielding team](/wiki/Fielding_team_(baseball) "Fielding team (baseball)"), called the [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher"), throws a [ball](/wiki/Baseball_(ball) "Baseball (ball)") that a player on the [batting team](/wiki/Batting_team_(baseball) "Batting team (baseball)"), called the [batter](/wiki/Batter_(baseball) "Batter (baseball)"), tries to hit with a [bat](/wiki/Baseball_bat "Baseball bat"). The objective of the offensive team (batting team) is to hit the ball into the field of play, away from the other team's players, allowing its players to run the [bases](/wiki/Base_(baseball) "Base (baseball)"), having them advance counter-

**Baseball** is a [bat-and-ball](/wiki/Bat-and-ball_games "Bat-and-ball games") [sport](/wiki/Sport "Sport") played between two [teams](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") of nine players each, taking turns [batting](/wiki/Batting_(baseball) "Batting (baseball)") and [fielding](/wiki/Fielding_(baseball) "Fielding (baseball)"). The game occurs over the course of several [plays](/wiki/Pitch_(baseball) "Pitch (baseball)"), with each play beginning when a player on the [fielding team](/wiki/Fielding_team_(baseball) "Fielding team (baseball)"), called the [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher"), throws a [ball](/wiki/Baseball_(ball) "Baseball (ball)") that a player on the [batting team](/wiki/Batting_team_(baseball) "Batting team (baseball)"), called the [batter](/wiki/Batter_(baseball) "Batter (baseball)"), tries to hit with a [bat](/wiki/Baseball_bat "Baseball bat"). The objective of the offensive team (batting team) is to hit the ball into the field of play, away from the other team's players, allowing its players to run the [bases](/wiki/Base_(baseball) "Base (baseball)"), having them advance counter-clockwise around four bases to score what are called "[runs](/wiki/Run_(baseball) "Run (baseball)")". The objective of the defensive team (referred to as the fielding team) is to prevent batters from becoming [runners](/wiki/Base_running "Base running"), and to prevent runners [advancing around the bases](/wiki/Base_running "Base running").[[2]](#cite_note-2) A run is scored when a runner legally advances around the bases in order and touches home plate (the place where the player started as a batter).

The initial objective of the batting team is to have a player reach first base [safely](/wiki/Safe_(baseball) "Safe (baseball)"); this occurs either when the batter hits the ball and reaches first base [before](/wiki/Force_play "Force play") an opponent retrieves the ball and touches the base, or when the pitcher persists in throwing the ball [out of the batter's reach](/wiki/Base_on_balls "Base on balls"). Players on the batting team who reach first base without being called "[out](/wiki/Out_(baseball) "Out (baseball)")" can attempt to advance to subsequent bases as a runner, either immediately or during teammates' turns batting. The fielding team tries to prevent runs by using the ball to get batters or runners "out", which forces them out of the field of play. The pitcher can get the batter out by throwing three [pitches](/wiki/Pitch_(baseball) "Pitch (baseball)") which result in [strikes](/wiki/Strike_(baseball) "Strike (baseball)"), while fielders can get the batter out by [catching](/wiki/Catch_(baseball) "Catch (baseball)") a batted ball before it touches the ground, and can get a runner out by [tagging](/wiki/Tag_out "Tag out") them with the ball while the runner is not touching a base.

The opposing teams switch back and forth between batting and fielding; the batting team's turn to bat is over once the fielding team records three outs. One turn batting for each team constitutes an [inning](/wiki/Inning "Inning"). A game is usually composed of nine innings, and the team with the greater number of runs at the end of the game wins. Most games end after the ninth inning, but if scores are tied at that point, [extra innings](/wiki/Extra_innings "Extra innings") are usually played. Baseball has no game clock, though some competitions feature [pace-of-play](/wiki/Pace_of_play "Pace of play") regulations such as a [pitch clock](/wiki/Pitch_clock "Pitch clock") to shorten game time.

Baseball [evolved from older bat-and-ball games](/wiki/Origins_of_baseball "Origins of baseball") already being played in [England](/wiki/England "England") by the mid-18th century. This game was brought by immigrants to North America, [where the modern version developed](/wiki/History_of_baseball_in_the_United_States "History of baseball in the United States"). Baseball's American origins, as well as its reputation as a source of [escapism](/wiki/Escapism "Escapism") during troubled points in American history such as the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") and the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), have led the sport to receive the moniker of "America's Pastime"; since the late 19th century, it has been unofficially recognized as the [national sport](/wiki/National_sport "National sport") of the [United States](/wiki/Baseball_in_the_United_States "Baseball in the United States"), though in modern times is considered [less popular than other sports](/wiki/Sports_in_the_United_States#Most_popular_sports_in_the_United_States "Sports in the United States"), such as [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football"). In addition to [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America"), baseball spread throughout the rest of the Americas and the Asia–Pacific in the 19th and 20th centuries,[[3]](#cite_note-3) and is now considered the most popular sport in parts of [Central](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"), and [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), particularly in [Japan](/wiki/Baseball_in_Japan "Baseball in Japan"), [South Korea](/wiki/Baseball_in_South_Korea "Baseball in South Korea"), and [Taiwan](/wiki/Sports_in_Taiwan#Baseball "Sports in Taiwan").

In [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB), the highest level of [professional baseball](/wiki/Professional_baseball "Professional baseball") in the United States and Canada, teams are divided into the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") (NL) and [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL), each with three divisions: East, West, and Central. The MLB champion is determined by [playoffs](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_postseason "Major League Baseball postseason") that culminate in the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"). The top level of play is similarly split in Japan between the [Central](/wiki/Central_League "Central League") and [Pacific Leagues](/wiki/Pacific_League "Pacific League") and in Cuba between the [West League and East League](/wiki/Cuban_National_Series "Cuban National Series"). The [World Baseball Classic](/wiki/World_Baseball_Classic "World Baseball Classic"), organized by the [World Baseball Softball Confederation](/wiki/World_Baseball_Softball_Confederation "World Baseball Softball Confederation"), is the major international competition of the sport and attracts the top national teams from around the world. Baseball was played [at the Olympic Games](/wiki/Baseball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Baseball at the Summer Olympics") from 1992 to 2008, and was reinstated on a one-off basis in 2020.

## Rules and gameplay

Further information: [Rules of baseball](/wiki/Rules_of_baseball "Rules of baseball") and [Outline of baseball](/wiki/Outline_of_baseball "Outline of baseball")

### Overview

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Baseball_diamond.svg/330px-Baseball_diamond.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Baseball_diamond.svg)

Diagram of a [baseball field](/wiki/Baseball_field "Baseball field"). *Diamond* may refer to the square area defined by the four bases or to the entire playing field. The dimensions given are for professional and professional-style games. Children often play on smaller fields.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/DR_vs_PR._World_Baseball_Classic.jpg/250px-DR_vs_PR._World_Baseball_Classic.jpg)](/wiki/File:DR_vs_PR._World_Baseball_Classic.jpg)

[2013 World Baseball Classic championship](/wiki/2013_World_Baseball_Classic_championship "2013 World Baseball Classic championship") match between the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic_national_baseball_team "Dominican Republic national baseball team") and [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico_national_baseball_team "Puerto Rico national baseball team"), March 20, 2013

A baseball game is played between two teams, each composed of nine players, that take turns playing offense (batting and baserunning) and defense (pitching and fielding). A pair of turns, one at bat and one in the field, by each team constitutes an [inning](/wiki/Inning "Inning"). A game consists of nine innings (seven innings at the high school level and in [doubleheaders](/wiki/Doubleheader_(baseball) "Doubleheader (baseball)") in college, [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") and, since the [2020 season](/wiki/2020_Major_League_Baseball_season "2020 Major League Baseball season"), [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"); and six innings at the Little League level).[[4]](#cite_note-League-4) One team—customarily the visiting team—bats in the top, or first half, of every inning. The other team—customarily the home team—bats in the bottom, or second half, of every inning.

The goal of the game is to score more points ([runs](/wiki/Run_(baseball) "Run (baseball)")) than the other team. The players on the team at bat attempt to score runs by touching all four bases, in order, set at the corners of the square-shaped [baseball diamond](/wiki/Baseball_field "Baseball field"). A player bats at [home plate](/wiki/Home_plate "Home plate") and must attempt to safely reach a base before proceeding, [counterclockwise](/wiki/Counterclockwise "Counterclockwise"), from first base, to second base, third base, and back home to score a run. The team in the field attempts to prevent runs from scoring by recording [outs](/wiki/Out_(baseball) "Out (baseball)"), which remove opposing players from offensive action until their next turn at bat comes up again. When three outs are recorded, the teams switch roles for the next half-inning. If the score of the game is tied after nine innings, [extra innings](/wiki/Extra_innings "Extra innings") are played to resolve the contest. Many amateur games, particularly unorganized ones, involve different numbers of players and innings.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The game is played on a field whose primary boundaries, the foul lines, extend forward from home plate at 45-degree angles. The 90-degree area within the foul lines is referred to as fair territory; the 270-degree area outside them is foul territory. The part of the field enclosed by the bases and several yards beyond them is the [infield](/wiki/Infield "Infield"); the area farther beyond the infield is the [outfield](/wiki/Outfield "Outfield"). In the middle of the infield is a raised pitcher's mound, with a rectangular rubber plate (the rubber) at its center. The outer boundary of the outfield is typically demarcated by a raised fence, which may be of any material and height. The fair territory between home plate and the outfield boundary is baseball's field of play, though significant events can take place in foul territory, as well.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

There are three basic tools of baseball: the [ball](/wiki/Baseball_(ball) "Baseball (ball)"), the [bat](/wiki/Baseball_bat "Baseball bat"), and the [glove or mitt](/wiki/Baseball_glove "Baseball glove"):

* The baseball is about the size of an adult's fist, around 9 inches (23 centimeters) in circumference. It has a rubber or cork center, wound in yarn and covered in white [cowhide](/wiki/Cowhide "Cowhide"), with red stitching.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* The bat is a hitting tool, traditionally made of a single, solid piece of wood. Other materials are now commonly used for nonprofessional games. It is a hard round stick, about 2.5 inches (6.4 centimeters) in diameter at the hitting end, tapering to a narrower handle and culminating in a knob. Bats used by adults are typically around 34 inches (86 centimeters) long, and not longer than 42 inches (110 centimeters).[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* The glove or mitt is a fielding tool, made of padded leather with webbing between the fingers. As an aid in catching and holding onto the ball, it takes various shapes to meet the specific needs of different fielding positions.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Protective [helmets](/wiki/Batting_helmet "Batting helmet") are also standard equipment for all batters.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Fielding positions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Baseball_positions_numbered_en.svg/250px-Baseball_positions_numbered_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Baseball_positions_numbered_en.svg)

Diagram indicating the standard layout of positions

At the beginning of each half-inning, the nine players of the fielding team arrange themselves around the field. One of them, the [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher"), stands on the pitcher's mound. The pitcher begins the pitching delivery with one foot on the rubber, pushing off it to gain velocity when throwing toward home plate. Another fielding team player, the [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher"), squats on the far side of home plate, facing the pitcher. The rest of the fielding team faces home plate, typically arranged as four infielders—who set up along or within a few yards outside the imaginary lines (basepaths) between first, second, and third base—and three outfielders. In the [standard arrangement](/wiki/Baseball_positions "Baseball positions"), there is a [first baseman](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman") positioned several steps to the left of first base, a [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman") to the right of second base, a [shortstop](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop") to the left of second base, and a [third baseman](/wiki/Third_baseman "Third baseman") to the right of third base. The basic outfield positions are [left fielder](/wiki/Left_fielder "Left fielder"), [center fielder](/wiki/Center_fielder "Center fielder"), and [right fielder](/wiki/Right_fielder "Right fielder"). With the exception of the catcher, all fielders are required to be in fair territory when the pitch is delivered. A neutral [umpire](/wiki/Umpire_(baseball) "Umpire (baseball)") sets up behind the catcher.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Other umpires will be distributed around the field as well.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Offense

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/David-ortiz-batters-box.JPG/250px-David-ortiz-batters-box.JPG)](/wiki/File:David-ortiz-batters-box.JPG)

[David Ortiz](/wiki/David_Ortiz "David Ortiz"), the batter, awaiting a pitch, with the catcher and umpire

Play starts with a member of the batting team, the batter, standing in either of the two [batter's boxes](/wiki/Batter%27s_box "Batter's box") next to home plate, holding a bat.[[13]](#cite_note-RulesPuttingBallInPlay-13) The batter waits for the pitcher to throw a pitch (the ball) toward home plate, and attempts to hit the ball[[14]](#cite_note-14) with the bat.[[13]](#cite_note-RulesPuttingBallInPlay-13) The catcher catches pitches that the batter does not hit—as a result of either electing not to swing or failing to connect—and returns them to the pitcher. A batter who hits the ball into the field of play must drop the bat and begin running toward first base, at which point the player is referred to as a *runner* (or, until the play is over, a *batter-runner*).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/5TH_3689_Lou_Marson.jpg/250px-5TH_3689_Lou_Marson.jpg)](/wiki/File:5TH_3689_Lou_Marson.jpg)

A runner sliding into home plate and scoring.

A batter-runner who reaches first base without being [put out](/wiki/Putout "Putout") is said to be *[safe](/wiki/Safe_(baseball) "Safe (baseball)")* and is on base. A batter-runner may choose to remain at first base or attempt to advance to second base or even beyond—however far the player believes can be reached safely. A player who reaches base despite proper play by the fielders has recorded a [hit](/wiki/Hit_(baseball) "Hit (baseball)"). A player who reaches first base safely on a hit is credited with a [single](/wiki/Single_(baseball) "Single (baseball)"). If a player makes it to second base safely as a direct result of a hit, it is a [double](/wiki/Double_(baseball) "Double (baseball)"); third base, a [triple](/wiki/Triple_(baseball) "Triple (baseball)"). If the ball is hit in the air within the foul lines over the entire outfield (and outfield fence, if there is one), or if the batter-runner otherwise safely circles all the bases, it is a [home run](/wiki/Home_run "Home run"): the batter and any runners on base may all freely circle the bases, each scoring a run. This is the most desirable result for the batter. The ultimate and most desirable result possible for a batter would be to hit a home run while all three bases are occupied or "loaded", thus scoring four runs on a single hit. This is called a [grand slam](/wiki/Grand_slam_(baseball) "Grand slam (baseball)"). A player who reaches base due to a fielding mistake is not credited with a hit—instead, the responsible fielder is charged with an [error](/wiki/Error_(baseball) "Error (baseball)").[[13]](#cite_note-RulesPuttingBallInPlay-13)

Any runners already on base may attempt to advance on batted balls that land, or contact the ground, in fair territory, before or after the ball lands. A runner on first base *must* attempt to advance if a ball lands in play, as only one runner may occupy a base at any given time; the same applies for other runners if they are on a base that a teammate is forced to advance to. If a ball hit into play rolls foul before passing through the infield, it becomes [dead](/wiki/Dead_ball_(baseball) "Dead ball (baseball)") and any runners must return to the base they occupied when the play began. If the ball is hit in the air and caught before it lands, the batter has [flied out](/wiki/Flyout "Flyout") and any runners on base may attempt to advance only if they [tag up](/wiki/Tag_up "Tag up") (contact the base they occupied when the play began, as or after the ball is caught). Runners may also attempt to advance to the next base while the pitcher is in the process of delivering the ball to home plate; a successful effort is a [stolen base](/wiki/Stolen_base "Stolen base").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Defense

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Strike_zone_en.JPG/250px-Strike_zone_en.JPG)](/wiki/File:Strike_zone_en.JPG)

The [strike zone](/wiki/Strike_zone "Strike zone") determines the result of most pitches, and varies in vertical length for each batter.

A pitch that is not hit into the field of play is called either a strike or a ball. A batter against whom three strikes are recorded [strikes out](/wiki/Strikeout "Strikeout"). A batter against whom four balls are recorded is awarded a [base on balls](/wiki/Base_on_balls "Base on balls") or walk, a free advance to first base. (A batter may also freely advance to first base if the batter's body or uniform is [struck by a pitch](/wiki/Hit_by_pitch "Hit by pitch") outside the strike zone, provided the batter does not swing and attempts to avoid being hit.)[[16]](#cite_note-16) Crucial to determining balls and strikes is the umpire's judgment as to whether a pitch has passed through the [strike zone](/wiki/Strike_zone "Strike zone"), a conceptual area above home plate extending from the midpoint between the batter's shoulders and belt down to the hollow of the knee.[[17]](#cite_note-MLBR2-17) Any pitch which does not pass through the strike zone is called a ball, unless the batter either swings and misses at the pitch, or hits the pitch into foul territory; an exception [generally](/wiki/Foul_tip "Foul tip") occurs if the ball is hit into foul territory when the batter already has two strikes, in which case neither a ball nor a strike is called.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Navy_baseball.jpg/250px-Navy_baseball.jpg)](/wiki/File:Navy_baseball.jpg)

A [shortstop](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop") tries to [tag out](/wiki/Tag_out "Tag out") a runner who is sliding head first, attempting to reach second base.

While the team at bat is trying to score runs, the team in the field is attempting to record outs. In addition to the strikeout and flyout, common ways a member of the batting team may be put out include the [ground out](/wiki/Ground_out_(baseball) "Ground out (baseball)"), [force out](/wiki/Force_play "Force play"), and [tag out](/wiki/Tag_out "Tag out"). These occur either when a runner is forced to advance to a base, and a fielder with possession of the ball reaches that base before the runner does, or the runner is touched by the ball, held in a fielder's hand, while not on a base. (The batter-runner is always forced to advance to first base, and any other runners must advance to the next base if a teammate is forced to advance to their base.) It is possible to record two outs in the course of the same play. This is called a [double play](/wiki/Double_play "Double play"). Three outs in one play, a [triple play](/wiki/Triple_play "Triple play"), is possible, though rare. Players put out or retired must leave the field, returning to their team's [dugout](/wiki/Dugout_(baseball) "Dugout (baseball)") or bench. A runner may be stranded on base when a third out is recorded against another player on the team. Stranded runners do not benefit the team in its next turn at bat as every half-inning begins with the bases empty.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Batting order and substitution

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Chris_Tillman%2C_Buck_Showalter_%2836615791150%29.jpg/250px-Chris_Tillman%2C_Buck_Showalter_%2836615791150%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chris_Tillman,_Buck_Showalter_(36615791150).jpg)

A pitcher handing off the ball after being taken out of the game during a [mound meeting](/wiki/Mound_meeting "Mound meeting").

An individual player's turn batting or [plate appearance](/wiki/Plate_appearance "Plate appearance") is complete when the player reaches base, hits a home run, makes an out, or hits a ball that results in the team's third out, even if it is recorded against a teammate. On rare occasions, a batter may be at the plate when, without the batter's hitting the ball, a third out is recorded against a teammate—for instance, a runner getting [caught stealing](/wiki/Caught_stealing "Caught stealing") (tagged out attempting to steal a base). A batter with this sort of incomplete plate appearance starts off the team's next turn batting; any balls or strikes recorded against the batter the previous inning are erased.

A runner may circle the bases only once per plate appearance and thus can score at most a single run per batting turn. Once a player has completed a plate appearance, that player may not bat again until the eight other members of the player's team have all taken their turn at bat in the [batting order](/wiki/Batting_order_(baseball) "Batting order (baseball)"). The batting order is set before the game begins, and may not be altered except for [substitutions](/wiki/Defensive_substitution "Defensive substitution"). Once a player has been removed for a substitute, that player may not reenter the game. Children's games often have more lenient rules, such as Little League rules, which allow players to be substituted back into the same game.[[4]](#cite_note-League-4)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

If the [designated hitter](/wiki/Designated_hitter "Designated hitter") (DH) rule is in effect, each team has a tenth player whose sole responsibility is to bat (and run). The DH takes the place of another player—almost invariably the pitcher—in the batting order, but does not field. Thus, even with the DH, each team still has a batting order of nine players and a fielding arrangement of nine players.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Personnel

See also: [Baseball positions](/wiki/Baseball_positions "Baseball positions")

### Players

See also the categories [Baseball players](/wiki/Category:Baseball_players "Category:Baseball players") and [Lists of baseball players](/wiki/Category:Lists_of_baseball_players "Category:Lists of baseball players")

The number of players on a baseball roster, or *squad*, varies by [league](/wiki/Baseball_league "Baseball league") and by the level of organized play. A [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) team has a roster of 26 players with specific roles. A typical roster features the following players:[[21]](#cite_note-21)

* Eight [position players](/wiki/Position_player "Position player"): the [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher"), four [infielders](/wiki/Infielder "Infielder"), and three [outfielders](/wiki/Outfielder "Outfielder")—all of whom play on a regular basis
* Five [starting pitchers](/wiki/Starting_pitcher "Starting pitcher") who constitute the team's [pitching](/wiki/Pitch_(baseball) "Pitch (baseball)") rotation or starting rotation
* Seven [relief pitchers](/wiki/Relief_pitcher "Relief pitcher"), including one [closer](/wiki/Closer_(baseball) "Closer (baseball)"), who constitute the team's [bullpen](/wiki/Bullpen "Bullpen") (named for the off-field area where pitchers warm up)
* One backup, or substitute, catcher
* Five backup infielders and backup outfielders, or players who can play multiple positions, known as [utility players](/wiki/Utility_player "Utility player").

Most baseball leagues worldwide have the DH rule, including MLB, [Japan's Pacific League](/wiki/Pacific_League "Pacific League"), and Caribbean professional leagues, along with major American amateur organizations.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The [Central League](/wiki/Central_League "Central League") in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") does not have the rule and high-level minor league clubs connected to National League teams are not required to field a DH.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In leagues that apply the designated hitter rule, a typical team has nine offensive regulars (including the DH), five starting pitchers,[[24]](#cite_note-24) seven or eight relievers, a backup catcher, and two or three other reserve players.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Managers and coaches

The [manager](/wiki/Manager_(baseball) "Manager (baseball)"), or head coach, oversees the team's major strategic decisions, such as establishing the starting rotation, setting the lineup, or batting order, before each game, and making substitutions during games—in particular, bringing in relief pitchers. Managers are typically assisted by two or more [coaches](/wiki/Coach_(baseball) "Coach (baseball)"); they may have specialized responsibilities, such as working with players on hitting, fielding, pitching, or strength and conditioning. At most levels of organized play, two coaches are stationed on the field when the team is at bat: the first base coach and third base coach, who occupy designated coaches' boxes, just outside the foul lines. These coaches assist in the direction of baserunners, when the ball is in play, and relay tactical signals from the manager to batters and runners, during pauses in play.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In contrast to many other team sports, baseball managers and coaches generally wear their team's uniforms; coaches must be in uniform to be allowed on the field to confer with players during a game.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Umpires

Any baseball game involves one or more [umpires](/wiki/Umpire_(baseball) "Umpire (baseball)"), who make rulings on the outcome of each play. At a minimum, one umpire will stand behind the catcher, to have a good view of the strike zone, and call balls and strikes. Additional umpires may be stationed near the other bases, thus making it easier to judge plays such as attempted force outs and tag outs. In MLB, four umpires are used for each game, one near each base. In the playoffs, six umpires are used: one at each base and two in the outfield along the foul lines.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Strategy

See also: [Baseball positioning](/wiki/Baseball_positioning "Baseball positioning")

Many of the pre-game and in-game strategic decisions in baseball revolve around a fundamental fact: in general, right-handed batters tend to be more successful against left-handed pitchers and, to an even greater degree, left-handed batters tend to be more successful against right-handed pitchers.[[30]](#cite_note-30) A manager with several left-handed batters in the regular lineup, who knows the team will be facing a left-handed starting pitcher, may respond by starting one or more of the right-handed backups on the team's roster. During the late innings of a game, as relief pitchers and pinch hitters are brought in, the opposing managers will often go back and forth trying to create favorable matchups with their substitutions. The manager of the fielding team trying to arrange same-handed pitcher-batter matchups and the manager of the batting team trying to arrange opposite-handed matchups. With a team that has the lead in the late innings, a manager may remove a starting position player—especially one whose turn at bat is not likely to come up again—for a more skillful fielder (known as a [defensive substitution](/wiki/Defensive_substitution "Defensive substitution")).[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Tactics

### Pitching and fielding

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Indiana_vs_Navy%2C_Baseball_pick-off_attempt.jpg/250px-Indiana_vs_Navy%2C_Baseball_pick-off_attempt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indiana_vs_Navy,_Baseball_pick-off_attempt.jpg)

A [first baseman](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman") receives a [pickoff](/wiki/Pickoff "Pickoff") throw, as the runner dives back to first base.

See also: [Pitch (baseball)](/wiki/Pitch_(baseball) "Pitch (baseball)")

The tactical decision that precedes almost every play in a baseball game involves pitch selection.[[32]](#cite_note-32) By gripping and then releasing the baseball in a certain manner, and by throwing it at a certain speed, pitchers can cause the baseball to break to either side, or downward, as it approaches the batter, thus creating differing pitches that can be selected.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Among the resulting wide variety of pitches that may be thrown, the four basic types are the [fastball](/wiki/Fastball "Fastball"), the [changeup](/wiki/Changeup "Changeup") (or off-speed pitch), and two [breaking balls](/wiki/Breaking_ball "Breaking ball")—the [curveball](/wiki/Curveball "Curveball") and the [slider](/wiki/Slider_(baseball) "Slider (baseball)").[[34]](#cite_note-34) Pitchers have different repertoires of pitches they are skillful at throwing. Conventionally, before each pitch, the catcher signals the pitcher what type of pitch to throw, as well as its general vertical or horizontal location.[[35]](#cite_note-35) If there is disagreement on the selection, the pitcher may [shake off the sign](/wiki/Glossary_of_baseball_terms#shake_off "Glossary of baseball terms") and the catcher will call for a different pitch.

With a runner on base and [taking a lead](/wiki/Glossary_of_baseball_terms#lead "Glossary of baseball terms"), the pitcher may attempt a [pickoff](/wiki/Pickoff "Pickoff"), a quick throw to a fielder [covering the base](/wiki/Covering_a_base "Covering a base") to keep the runner's lead in check or, optimally, effect a tag out.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Pickoff attempts, however, are subject to rules that severely restrict the pitcher's movements before and during the pickoff attempt. Violation of any one of these rules could result in the umpire calling a [balk](/wiki/Balk "Balk") against the pitcher, which permits any runners on base to advance one base with impunity.[[37]](#cite_note-37) If an attempted [stolen base](/wiki/Stolen_base "Stolen base") is anticipated, the catcher may call for a [pitchout](/wiki/Pitchout "Pitchout"), a ball thrown deliberately off the plate, allowing the catcher to catch it while standing and throw quickly to a base.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Facing a batter with a strong tendency to hit to one side of the field, the fielding team may employ a [shift](/wiki/Glossary_of_baseball_terms#shift "Glossary of baseball terms"), with most or all of the fielders moving to the left or right of their usual positions. With a runner on third base, the infielders may [play in](/wiki/Glossary_of_baseball_terms#playing_in "Glossary of baseball terms"), moving closer to home plate to improve the odds of throwing out the runner on a [ground ball](/wiki/Glossary_of_baseball_terms#ground_ball "Glossary of baseball terms"), though a sharply hit grounder is more likely to carry through a drawn-in infield.[[39]](#cite_note-SB45-39)

### Batting and baserunning

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Mookie_Betts_hitting_the_ball_%2836478781664%29.jpg/250px-Mookie_Betts_hitting_the_ball_%2836478781664%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mookie_Betts_hitting_the_ball_(36478781664).jpg)

[Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") player [Mookie Betts](/wiki/Mookie_Betts "Mookie Betts") hits a pitch by swinging his [bat](/wiki/Baseball_bat "Baseball bat").

Several basic offensive tactics come into play with a runner on first base, including the fundamental choice of whether to attempt a steal of second base. The [hit and run](/wiki/Hit_and_run_(baseball) "Hit and run (baseball)") is sometimes employed, with a skillful [contact hitter](/wiki/Contact_hitter "Contact hitter"), the runner takes off with the pitch, drawing the shortstop or second baseman over to second base, creating a gap in the infield for the batter to poke the ball through.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The [sacrifice bunt](/wiki/Sacrifice_bunt "Sacrifice bunt"), calls for the batter to focus on making soft contact with the ball, so that it rolls a short distance into the infield, allowing the runner to advance into [scoring position](/wiki/Scoring_position "Scoring position") as the batter is thrown out at first. A batter, particularly one who is a fast runner, may also attempt to [bunt](/wiki/Bunt_(baseball) "Bunt (baseball)") for a hit. A sacrifice bunt employed with a runner on third base, aimed at bringing that runner home, is known as a [squeeze play](/wiki/Squeeze_play_(baseball) "Squeeze play (baseball)").[[41]](#cite_note-41) With a runner on third and fewer than two outs, a batter may instead concentrate on hitting a fly ball that, even if it is caught, will be deep enough to allow the runner to tag up and score—a successful batter, in this case, gets credit for a [sacrifice fly](/wiki/Sacrifice_fly "Sacrifice fly").[[39]](#cite_note-SB45-39) In order to increase the chance of advancing a batter to first base via a walk, the manager will sometimes signal a batter who is [ahead in the count](/wiki/Glossary_of_baseball_terms#ahead_in_the_count "Glossary of baseball terms") (i.e., has more balls than strikes) to [take](/wiki/Glossary_of_baseball_terms#take_sign "Glossary of baseball terms"), or not swing at, the next pitch. The batter's potential reward of reaching base (via a walk) exceeds the disadvantage if the next pitch is a strike.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## History

Main article: [History of baseball](/wiki/History_of_baseball "History of baseball")

Further information: [Origins of baseball](/wiki/Origins_of_baseball "Origins of baseball")

Consensus once held that today's baseball is a North American development from the older game [rounders](/wiki/Rounders "Rounders"), popular among children in [Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland").[[43]](#cite_note-BlockRader-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) American baseball historian David Block suggests that the game originated in [England](/wiki/England "England"); recently uncovered historical evidence supports this position. According to Block and official MLB historian [John Thorn](/wiki/John_Thorn_(baseball_historian) "John Thorn (baseball historian)"), this earlier version of baseball may have involved hitting the ball with a hand, making it akin to today's [punchball](/wiki/Punchball "Punchball").[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) Block argues that rounders and early baseball were actually regional variants of each other, and that the game's most direct antecedents are the English games of [stoolball](/wiki/Stoolball "Stoolball") and "tut-ball".[[43]](#cite_note-BlockRader-43) The earliest known reference to baseball is in a 1744 British publication, *[A Little Pretty Pocket-Book](/wiki/A_Little_Pretty_Pocket-Book "A Little Pretty Pocket-Book")*, by [John Newbery](/wiki/John_Newbery "John Newbery").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Block discovered that the first recorded game of "Base Ball"[[49]](#cite_note-49) took place in 1749 in [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey"), and featured the [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Frederick,_Prince_of_Wales "Frederick, Prince of Wales") as a player.[[50]](#cite_note-50) This early form of the game was apparently brought to [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") by English immigrants.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Cricket_vs._baseball_%284050380395%29.jpg/250px-Cricket_vs._baseball_%284050380395%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cricket_vs._baseball_(4050380395).jpg)

By the 1860s [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), baseball (bottom) had overtaken its fellow bat-and-ball sport [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") (top) in popularity within the United States.[[a]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) Growing American influence abroad meant the same occurred in Japan and the Dominican Republic by the early 20th century.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)

By the early 1830s, there were reports of a variety of uncodified bat-and-ball games recognizable as early forms of baseball being played around North America.[[56]](#cite_note-57) The first recorded baseball game in North America was played in [Beachville, Ontario](/wiki/Beachville,_Ontario "Beachville, Ontario"), Canada, on June 4, 1838.[[57]](#cite_note-58) In 1845, [Alexander Cartwright](/wiki/Alexander_Cartwright "Alexander Cartwright"), a member of New York City's [Knickerbocker Club](/wiki/Knickerbocker_Base_Ball_Club "Knickerbocker Base Ball Club"), led the codification of the so-called [Knickerbocker Rules](/wiki/Knickerbocker_Rules "Knickerbocker Rules"),[[58]](#cite_note-59) which in turn were based on rules developed in 1837 by [William R. Wheaton](/wiki/William_R._Wheaton "William R. Wheaton") of the Gotham Club.[[59]](#cite_note-60) While there are reports that the [New York Knickerbockers](/wiki/Knickerbocker_Base_Ball_Club_of_New_York "Knickerbocker Base Ball Club of New York") played games in 1845, the contest long recognized as the first recorded baseball game in U.S. history took place on June 19, 1846, in [Hoboken, New Jersey](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey"): the "New York Nine" defeated the Knickerbockers, 23–1, in four innings.[[60]](#cite_note-61) With the Knickerbocker code as the basis, the rules of modern baseball continued to evolve over the next half-century.[[61]](#cite_note-62) The game then went on to spread throughout the [Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim "Pacific Rim") and the Americas,[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64) with Americans backing the sport as a way to spread American values.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

### In the United States

Further information: [Baseball in the United States](/wiki/Baseball_in_the_United_States "Baseball in the United States") and [History of baseball in the United States](/wiki/History_of_baseball_in_the_United_States "History of baseball in the United States")

#### Establishment of professional leagues

In the mid-1850s, a baseball craze hit the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"),[[65]](#cite_note-66) and by 1856, local journals were referring to baseball as the "national pastime" or "national game".[[66]](#cite_note-67) A year later, the sport's first governing body, the [National Association of Base Ball Players](/wiki/National_Association_of_Base_Ball_Players "National Association of Base Ball Players"), was formed. In 1867, it barred participation by [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans").[[67]](#cite_note-68) The more formally structured [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") was founded in 1876.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Professional [Negro leagues](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") formed, but quickly folded.[[69]](#cite_note-70) In 1887, [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball"), under the name of indoor baseball or indoor-outdoor, was invented as a winter version of the parent game.[[70]](#cite_note-71) The National League's first successful counterpart, the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), which evolved from the minor [Western League](/wiki/Western_League_(original) "Western League (original)"), was established in 1893, and virtually all of the modern [baseball rules](/wiki/Baseball_rules "Baseball rules") were in place by then.[[71]](#cite_note-R71-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

The National Agreement of 1903 formalized relations both between the two major leagues and between them and the National Association of Professional Base Ball Leagues, representing most of the country's [minor professional leagues](/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball").[[73]](#cite_note-74) The [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), pitting the two major league champions against each other, was inaugurated that fall.[[74]](#cite_note-75) The [Black Sox Scandal](/wiki/Black_Sox_Scandal "Black Sox Scandal") of the [1919 World Series](/wiki/1919_World_Series "1919 World Series") led to the formation of the office of the [Commissioner of Baseball](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Baseball "Commissioner of Baseball").[[75]](#cite_note-76) The first commissioner, [Kenesaw Mountain Landis](/wiki/Kenesaw_Mountain_Landis "Kenesaw Mountain Landis"), was elected in 1920. That year also saw the founding of the [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1920%E2%80%931931) "Negro National League (1920–1931)"); the first significant Negro league, it would operate until 1931. For part of the 1920s, it was joined by the [Eastern Colored League](/wiki/Eastern_Colored_League "Eastern Colored League").[[76]](#cite_note-77)

#### Rise of Ruth and racial integration

Compared with the present, professional baseball in the early 20th century was lower-scoring, and pitchers were more dominant.[[77]](#cite_note-78) This so-called "[dead-ball era](/wiki/Dead-ball_era "Dead-ball era")" ended in the early 1920s with several changes in rule and circumstance that were advantageous to hitters. Strict new regulations governed the ball's size, shape and composition, along with a new rule officially banning the [spitball](/wiki/Spitball "Spitball") and other pitches that depended on the ball being treated or roughed-up with foreign substances, resulted in a ball that traveled farther when hit.[[78]](#cite_note-79) The rise of the legendary player [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth"), the first great power hitter of the new era, helped permanently alter the nature of the game.[[79]](#cite_note-80) In the late 1920s and early 1930s, [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") general manager [Branch Rickey](/wiki/Branch_Rickey "Branch Rickey") invested in several [minor league clubs](/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball") and developed the first modern [farm system](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team").[[80]](#cite_note-81) A new [Negro National League](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1933%E2%80%931948) "Negro National League (1933–1948)") was organized in 1933; four years later, it was joined by the [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League"). The [first elections](/wiki/Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_balloting,_1936 "Baseball Hall of Fame balloting, 1936") to the [National Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame_and_Museum "National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum") took place in 1936. In 1939, [Little League Baseball](/wiki/Little_League_Baseball "Little League Baseball") was founded in Pennsylvania.[[81]](#cite_note-82)

[![Robinson posing in the uniform cap of the Kansas City Monarchs, a Negro League baseball team, 1945](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Jackie_Robinson_Kansas_City_Monarchs.jpg/250px-Jackie_Robinson_Kansas_City_Monarchs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jackie_Robinson_Kansas_City_Monarchs.jpg)

[Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") in 1945, with the [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League")'s [Kansas City Monarchs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Monarchs "Kansas City Monarchs")

Many minor league teams disbanded when [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") led to a player shortage. [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") owner [Philip K. Wrigley](/wiki/Philip_K._Wrigley "Philip K. Wrigley") led the formation of the [All-American Girls Professional Baseball League](/wiki/All-American_Girls_Professional_Baseball_League "All-American Girls Professional Baseball League") to help keep the game in the public eye.[[82]](#cite_note-83) The first crack in the unwritten agreement barring blacks from white-controlled professional ball occurred in 1945: [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") was signed by the National League's [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") and began playing for their minor league [team in Montreal](/wiki/Montreal_Royals "Montreal Royals").[[83]](#cite_note-84) In 1947, Robinson broke the major leagues' color barrier when he debuted with the Dodgers.[[84]](#cite_note-85) Latin-American players, largely overlooked before, also started entering the majors in greater numbers. In 1951, two Chicago White Sox, Venezuelan-born [Chico Carrasquel](/wiki/Chico_Carrasquel "Chico Carrasquel") and black Cuban-born [Minnie Miñoso](/wiki/Minnie_Mi%C3%B1oso "Minnie Miñoso"), became the first Hispanic [All-Stars](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "Major League Baseball All-Star Game").[[85]](#cite_note-P111-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87) [Integration](/wiki/Racial_integration "Racial integration") proceeded slowly: by 1953, only six of the 16 major league teams had a black player on the roster.[[85]](#cite_note-P111-86)

#### Attendance records and the age of steroids

In 1975, the union's power—and players' salaries—began to increase greatly when the reserve clause was [effectively struck down](/wiki/Seitz_decision "Seitz decision"), leading to the [free agency system](/wiki/Free_agent "Free agent").[[87]](#cite_note-88) Significant work stoppages occurred in [1981](/wiki/1981_Major_League_Baseball_strike "1981 Major League Baseball strike") and [1994](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Major_League_Baseball_strike "1994–95 Major League Baseball strike"), the latter forcing the cancellation of the World Series for the first time in 90 years.[[88]](#cite_note-89) Attendance had been growing steadily since the mid-1970s and in 1994, before the stoppage, the majors were setting their all-time record for per-game attendance.[[89]](#cite_note-Simm-90)[[90]](#cite_note-91) After play resumed in 1995, non-division-winning [wild card](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports) "Wild card (sports)") teams became a permanent fixture of the post-season. Regular-season [interleague play](/wiki/Interleague_play "Interleague play") was introduced in 1997 and the second-highest attendance mark for a full season was set.[[91]](#cite_note-92) In 2000, the National and American Leagues were dissolved as legal entities. While their identities were maintained for scheduling purposes (and the designated hitter distinction), the regulations and other functions—such as player discipline and [umpire](/wiki/Umpire_(baseball) "Umpire (baseball)") supervision—they had administered separately were consolidated under the rubric of MLB.[[92]](#cite_note-93)

In 2001, [Barry Bonds](/wiki/Barry_Bonds "Barry Bonds") established the current record of 73 home runs in a single season. There had long been suspicions that the dramatic increase in power hitting was fueled in large part by [the abuse of illegal steroids](/wiki/Banned_substances_in_baseball "Banned substances in baseball") (as well as by the dilution of pitching talent due to expansion), but the issue only began attracting significant media attention in 2002 and there was no penalty for the use of performance-enhancing drugs before 2004.[[93]](#cite_note-94) In 2007, Bonds became MLB's all-time home run leader, surpassing [Hank Aaron](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron"), as total major league and minor league attendance both reached all-time highs.[[94]](#cite_note-MajorAtRec-95)[[95]](#cite_note-MinorAtRec-96)

### Around the world

Main article: [History of baseball outside the United States](/wiki/History_of_baseball_outside_the_United_States "History of baseball outside the United States")

Despite having been called "America's national pastime", baseball is well-established in several other countries. As early as 1877, a professional league, the [International Association](/wiki/International_Association_for_Professional_Base_Ball_Players "International Association for Professional Base Ball Players"), featured teams from both Canada and the United States.[[96]](#cite_note-97) While baseball is widely played in Canada and many minor league teams have been based in the country,[[97]](#cite_note-98)[[98]](#cite_note-99) the American major leagues did not include a Canadian club until 1969, when the [Montreal Expos](/wiki/Montreal_Expos "Montreal Expos") joined the National League as an expansion team. In 1977, the expansion [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") joined the American League.[[99]](#cite_note-100)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/WBC2006_Sadaharu_Oh.jpg/250px-WBC2006_Sadaharu_Oh.jpg)](/wiki/File:WBC2006_Sadaharu_Oh.jpg)

[Sadaharu Oh](/wiki/Sadaharu_Oh "Sadaharu Oh") managing the [Japan national team](/wiki/Japan_national_baseball_team "Japan national baseball team") in the [2006 World Baseball Classic](/wiki/2006_World_Baseball_Classic "2006 World Baseball Classic"). Playing for the [Central League](/wiki/Central_League "Central League")'s [Yomiuri Giants](/wiki/Yomiuri_Giants "Yomiuri Giants") (1959–80), Oh set the professional world record for home runs.

In 1847, American soldiers played what may have been the first baseball game in Mexico at [Parque Los Berros](/wiki/Parque_Los_Berros "Parque Los Berros") in [Xalapa](/wiki/Xalapa "Xalapa"), [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz "Veracruz").[[100]](#cite_note-101) The first formal baseball league outside of the United States and Canada was founded in 1878 in Cuba, which maintains a rich baseball tradition. The [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Baseball_in_Dominican_Republic "Baseball in Dominican Republic") held its first islandwide championship tournament in 1912.[[101]](#cite_note-102) Professional baseball tournaments and leagues began to form in other countries between the world wars, including the Netherlands (formed in 1922), Australia (1934), Japan (1936), Mexico (1937), and Puerto Rico (1938).[[102]](#cite_note-103) The [Japanese major leagues](/wiki/Nippon_Professional_Baseball "Nippon Professional Baseball") have long been considered the highest quality professional circuits outside of the United States.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Eino_Kaakkolahti.jpg/250px-Eino_Kaakkolahti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eino_Kaakkolahti.jpg)

[Pesäpallo](/wiki/Pes%C3%A4pallo "Pesäpallo"), a [Finnish](/wiki/Finland "Finland") variation of baseball, was invented by [Lauri "Tahko" Pihkala](/wiki/Lauri_Pihkala "Lauri Pihkala") in the 1920s,[[104]](#cite_note-105) and after that, it has changed with the times and grown in popularity. Picture of Pesäpallo match in 1958 in [Jyväskylä](/wiki/Jyv%C3%A4skyl%C3%A4 "Jyväskylä"), Finland.

After World War II, professional leagues were founded in many Latin American countries, most prominently [Venezuela](/wiki/Baseball_in_Venezuela "Baseball in Venezuela") (1946) and the Dominican Republic (1955).[[105]](#cite_note-106) Since the early 1970s, the annual [Caribbean Series](/wiki/Caribbean_Series "Caribbean Series") has matched the championship clubs from the four leading Latin American winter leagues: the [Dominican Professional Baseball League](/wiki/Dominican_Professional_Baseball_League "Dominican Professional Baseball League"), [Mexican Pacific League](/wiki/Mexican_Pacific_League "Mexican Pacific League"), [Puerto Rican Professional Baseball League](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_Professional_Baseball_League "Puerto Rican Professional Baseball League"), and [Venezuelan Professional Baseball League](/wiki/Venezuelan_Professional_Baseball_League "Venezuelan Professional Baseball League"). In Asia, South Korea (1982), Taiwan (1990) and China (2003) all have professional leagues.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

The English football club, [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa_F.C. "Aston Villa F.C."), were the first British baseball champions winning the [1890 National League of Baseball of Great Britain](/wiki/1890_National_League_of_Baseball_of_Great_Britain "1890 National League of Baseball of Great Britain").[[107]](#cite_note-108)[[108]](#cite_note-Cheats-109) The 2025 [National Champions](/wiki/Baseball_in_the_United_Kingdom#National_Baseball_Champions "Baseball in the United Kingdom") were the [London Mets](/wiki/London_Mets "London Mets"). Other European countries have seen professional leagues; the most successful, other than the [Dutch league](/wiki/Honkbal_Hoofdklasse "Honkbal Hoofdklasse"), is the [Italian league](/wiki/Serie_A1_(baseball) "Serie A1 (baseball)"), founded in 1948.[[109]](#cite_note-110) In 2004, Australia won a surprise silver medal at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/2004_Olympic_Games "2004 Olympic Games").[[110]](#cite_note-111) The Confédération Européene de Baseball (European Baseball Confederation), founded in 1953, organizes a number of competitions between clubs from different countries. Other competitions between national teams, such as the [Baseball World Cup](/wiki/Baseball_World_Cup "Baseball World Cup") and the [Olympic baseball tournament](/wiki/Baseball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Baseball at the Summer Olympics"), were administered by the [International Baseball Federation](/wiki/International_Baseball_Federation "International Baseball Federation") (IBAF) from its formation in 1938 until its 2013 merger with the [International Softball Federation](/wiki/International_Softball_Federation "International Softball Federation") to create the current joint governing body for both sports, the [World Baseball Softball Confederation](/wiki/World_Baseball_Softball_Confederation "World Baseball Softball Confederation") (WBSC).[[111]](#cite_note-112) [Women's baseball](/wiki/Women%27s_baseball "Women's baseball") is played on an organized amateur basis in numerous countries.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

After being admitted to the Olympics as a [medal sport](/wiki/Olympic_sport "Olympic sport") beginning with the [1992 Games](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics"), baseball was dropped from the [2012 Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympic_Games "2012 Summer Olympic Games") at the 2005 [International Olympic Committee meeting](/wiki/117th_IOC_Session "117th IOC Session"). It remained part of the [2008 Games](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympic_Games "2008 Summer Olympic Games").[[113]](#cite_note-114) While the sport's lack of a following in much of the world was a factor,[[114]](#cite_note-115) more important was MLB's reluctance to allow its players to participate during the major league season.[[115]](#cite_note-116) MLB initiated the [World Baseball Classic](/wiki/World_Baseball_Classic "World Baseball Classic"), scheduled to precede its season, partly as a replacement, high-profile international tournament. The [inaugural Classic](/wiki/2006_World_Baseball_Classic "2006 World Baseball Classic"), held in March 2006, was the first tournament involving national teams to feature a significant number of MLB participants.[[116]](#cite_note-117)[[117]](#cite_note-118) The Baseball World Cup was discontinued after its [2011 edition](/wiki/2011_Baseball_World_Cup "2011 Baseball World Cup") in favor of an expanded World Baseball Classic.[[118]](#cite_note-119)

## Distinctive elements

Baseball has certain attributes that set it apart from the other popular team sports in the countries where it has a following. All of these sports use a clock,[[119]](#cite_note-120) play is less individual,[[120]](#cite_note-Mandelbaum5557-121) and the variation between playing fields is not as substantial or important.[[121]](#cite_note-122) The [comparison between cricket and baseball](/wiki/Comparison_between_cricket_and_baseball "Comparison between cricket and baseball") demonstrates that many of baseball's distinctive elements are shared in various ways with similar sports.[[122]](#cite_note-123)[[123]](#cite_note-124)

### No clock to kill

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/A_worn-out_baseball.JPG/250px-A_worn-out_baseball.JPG)](/wiki/File:A_worn-out_baseball.JPG)

A well-worn baseball

In clock-limited sports, games often end with a team that holds the lead [killing the clock](/wiki/Stalling_(gaming) "Stalling (gaming)") rather than competing aggressively against the opposing team. In contrast, baseball has no clock, thus a team cannot win without getting the last batter out and rallies are not constrained by time. At almost any turn in any baseball game, the most advantageous strategy is some form of aggressive strategy.[[124]](#cite_note-125) Whereas, in the case of multi-day [Test](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") and [first-class cricket](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket"), the possibility of a [draw](/wiki/Draw_(cricket) "Draw (cricket)") (which occurs because of the restrictions on time, which like in baseball, originally [did not exist](/wiki/Timeless_Test "Timeless Test")[[125]](#cite_note-126)) often encourages a team that is batting last and well behind, to bat defensively and run out the clock, giving up any faint chance at a win, to avoid an overall loss.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

While nine innings has been the standard since the beginning of professional baseball, the duration of the average major league game has increased steadily through the years. At the turn of the 20th century, games typically took an hour and a half to play. In the 1920s, they averaged just less than two hours, which eventually ballooned to 2:38 in 1960.[[127]](#cite_note-HB-128) By 1997, the average American League game lasted 2:57 (National League games were about 10 minutes shorter—pitchers at the plate making for quicker outs than designated hitters).[[128]](#cite_note-JG-129) In 2004, Major League Baseball declared that its goal was an average game of 2:45.[[127]](#cite_note-HB-128) By 2014, though, the average MLB game took over three hours to complete.[[129]](#cite_note-130) The lengthening of games is attributed to longer breaks between half-innings for television commercials, increased offense, more pitching changes, and a slower pace of play, with pitchers taking more time between each delivery, and batters stepping out of the box more frequently.[[127]](#cite_note-HB-128)[[128]](#cite_note-JG-129) Other leagues have experienced similar issues. In 2008, [Nippon Professional Baseball](/wiki/Nippon_Professional_Baseball "Nippon Professional Baseball") took steps aimed at shortening games by 12 minutes from the preceding decade's average of 3:18.[[130]](#cite_note-131)

In 2016, the average nine-inning playoff game in Major League baseball was 3 hours and 35 minutes. This was up 10 minutes from 2015 and 21 minutes from 2014.[[131]](#cite_note-132) In response to the lengthening of the game, MLB decided from the 2023 season onward to institute a [pitch clock](/wiki/Pitch_clock "Pitch clock") rule to penalize batters and pitchers who take too much time between pitches; this had the effect of shortening 2023 regular season games by 24 minutes on average.[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134)

### Individual focus

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Babe_Ruth2.jpg/250px-Babe_Ruth2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Babe_Ruth2.jpg)

[Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth") in 1920, the year he joined the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees")

Although baseball is a team sport, individual players are often placed under scrutiny and pressure. While rewarding, it has sometimes been described as "ruthless" due to the pressure on the individual player.[[134]](#cite_note-135) In 1915, a baseball instructional manual pointed out that every single pitch, of which there are often more than two hundred in a game, involves an individual, one-on-one contest: "the pitcher and the batter in a battle of wits".[[135]](#cite_note-136) Pitcher, batter, and fielder all act essentially independent of each other. While coaching staffs can signal pitcher or batter to pursue certain tactics, the execution of the play itself is a series of solitary acts. If the batter hits a line drive, the outfielder is solely responsible for deciding to try to catch it or play it on the bounce and for succeeding or failing. The [statistical precision of baseball](/wiki/Baseball_statistics "Baseball statistics") is both facilitated by this isolation and reinforces it.

Cricket is more similar to baseball than many other team sports in this regard: while the individual focus in cricket is mitigated by the importance of the [batting partnership](/wiki/Partnership_(cricket) "Partnership (cricket)") and the practicalities of tandem running, it is enhanced by the fact that a batsman may occupy the [wicket](/wiki/Wicket "Wicket") for an hour or much more.[[136]](#cite_note-137) There is no statistical equivalent in cricket for the fielding error and thus less emphasis on personal responsibility in this area of play.[[137]](#cite_note-138)

### Uniqueness of parks

Further information: [Ballpark](/wiki/Ballpark "Ballpark")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Fenway_Park_-_Oct_5th%2C_2021_-_ALCS_Wild_Card.jpg/250px-Fenway_Park_-_Oct_5th%2C_2021_-_ALCS_Wild_Card.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fenway_Park_-_Oct_5th,_2021_-_ALCS_Wild_Card.jpg)

[Fenway Park](/wiki/Fenway_Park "Fenway Park"), home of the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox"). The [Green Monster](/wiki/Green_Monster "Green Monster") is visible beyond the playing field on the left.

Unlike those of most sports, baseball playing fields can vary significantly in size and shape.[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140) While the dimensions of the infield are specifically regulated, the only constraint on outfield size and shape for professional teams, following the rules of MLB and [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball"), is that fields built or remodeled since June 1, 1958, must have a minimum distance of 325 feet (99 m) from home plate to the fences in left and right field and 400 feet (122 m) to center.[[140]](#cite_note-141) Major league teams often skirt even this rule. For example, at [Daikin Park](/wiki/Daikin_Park "Daikin Park"), which became the home of the [Houston Astros](/wiki/Houston_Astros "Houston Astros") in 2000, the [Crawford Boxes](/wiki/Crawford_Boxes "Crawford Boxes") in left field are only 315 feet (96 m) from home plate.[[141]](#cite_note-142) There are no rules at all that address the height of fences or other structures at the edge of the outfield. The most famously idiosyncratic outfield boundary is the left-field wall at Boston's [Fenway Park](/wiki/Fenway_Park "Fenway Park"), in use since 1912: the [Green Monster](/wiki/Green_Monster "Green Monster") is 310 feet (94 m) from home plate down the line and 37 feet (11 m) tall.[[142]](#cite_note-143)

Similarly, there are no regulations at all concerning the dimensions of foul territory. Thus a foul fly ball may be entirely out of play in a park with little space between the foul lines and the stands, but a foulout in a park with more expansive foul ground.[[143]](#cite_note-144) A fence in foul territory that is close to the outfield line will tend to direct balls that strike it back toward the fielders, while one that is farther away may actually prompt more collisions, as outfielders run full speed to field balls deep in the corner. These variations can make the difference between a double and a triple or [inside-the-park home run](/wiki/Inside-the-park_home_run "Inside-the-park home run").[[144]](#cite_note-145) The surface of the field is also unregulated. While the adjacent image shows a traditional field surfacing arrangement (and the one used by virtually all MLB teams with naturally surfaced fields), teams are free to decide what areas will be grassed or bare.[[145]](#cite_note-146) Some fields—including several in MLB—use [artificial turf](/wiki/Artificial_turf "Artificial turf"). Surface variations can have a significant effect on how ground balls behave and are fielded as well as on baserunning. Similarly, the presence of a roof (seven major league teams play in stadiums with permanent or retractable roofs) can greatly affect how fly balls are played.[[146]](#cite_note-147) While football and soccer players deal with similar variations of field surface and stadium covering, the size and shape of their fields are much more standardized. The area out-of-bounds on a football or soccer field does not affect play the way foul territory in baseball does, so variations in that regard are largely insignificant.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Red_Sox_Yankees_Game_Boston_July_2012.jpg/250px-Red_Sox_Yankees_Game_Boston_July_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Red_Sox_Yankees_Game_Boston_July_2012.jpg)

A [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") batter ([Andruw Jones](/wiki/Andruw_Jones "Andruw Jones")) and a [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") catcher at [Fenway Park](/wiki/Fenway_Park "Fenway Park")

These physical variations create a distinctive set of playing conditions at each ballpark. Other local factors, such as altitude and climate, can also significantly affect play. A given stadium may acquire a reputation as a pitcher's park or a hitter's park, if one or the other discipline notably benefits from its unique mix of elements. The most exceptional park in this regard is [Coors Field](/wiki/Coors_Field "Coors Field"), home of the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies"). Its high altitude—5,282 feet (1,610 m) above sea level—is partly responsible for giving it the strongest hitter's park effect in the major leagues due to the low air pressure.[[148]](#cite_note-149) [Wrigley Field](/wiki/Wrigley_Field "Wrigley Field"), home of the Chicago Cubs, is known for its fickle disposition: a pitcher's park when the strong winds off [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") are blowing in, it becomes more of a hitter's park when they are blowing out.[[149]](#cite_note-150) The absence of a standardized field affects not only how particular games play out, but the nature of team rosters and players' statistical records. For example, hitting a fly ball 330 feet (100 m) into right field might result in an easy catch on the [warning track](/wiki/Warning_track "Warning track") at one park, and a home run at another. A team that plays in a park with a relatively short right field, such as the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"), will tend to stock its roster with left-handed [pull hitters](/wiki/Pull_hitter "Pull hitter"), who can best exploit it. On the individual level, a player who spends most of his career with a team that plays in a hitter's park will gain an advantage in batting statistics over time—even more so if his talents are especially suited to the park.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

## Statistics

Further information: [Baseball statistics](/wiki/Baseball_statistics "Baseball statistics")

Organized baseball lends itself to [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") to a greater degree than many other sports. Each play is discrete and has a relatively small number of possible outcomes. In the late 19th century, a former cricket player, English-born [Henry Chadwick](/wiki/Henry_Chadwick_(writer) "Henry Chadwick (writer)") of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), was responsible for the "development of the [box score](/wiki/Box_score_(baseball) "Box score (baseball)"), tabular standings, the annual baseball guide, the [batting average](/wiki/Batting_average_(baseball) "Batting average (baseball)"), and most of the common statistics and tables used to describe baseball."[[151]](#cite_note-T16-152) The statistical record is so central to the game's "historical essence" that Chadwick came to be known as Father Baseball.[[151]](#cite_note-T16-152) In the 1920s, American newspapers began devoting more and more attention to baseball statistics, initiating what journalist and historian [Alan Schwarz](/wiki/Alan_Schwarz "Alan Schwarz") describes as a "tectonic shift in sports, as intrigue that once focused mostly on teams began to go to individual players and their statistics lines."[[152]](#cite_note-153)

The Official Baseball Rules administered by MLB require the [official scorer](/wiki/Official_scorer "Official scorer") to categorize each baseball play unambiguously. The rules provide detailed criteria to promote consistency. The [score report](/wiki/Baseball_scorekeeping "Baseball scorekeeping") is the official basis for both the box score of the game and the relevant statistical records.[[153]](#cite_note-154) General managers, managers, and [baseball scouts](/wiki/Scout_(sport) "Scout (sport)") use statistics to evaluate players and make strategic decisions.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Baseball_steal.jpg/250px-Baseball_steal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baseball_steal.jpg)

[Rickey Henderson](/wiki/Rickey_Henderson "Rickey Henderson")—the major leagues' all-time leader in runs and stolen bases—stealing third base in a 1988 game

Certain traditional statistics are familiar to most baseball fans. The basic batting statistics include:[[154]](#cite_note-155)

* [At bats](/wiki/At_bat "At bat"): plate appearances, excluding walks and [hit by pitches](/wiki/Hit_by_pitch "Hit by pitch")—where the batter's ability is not fully tested—and sacrifices and sacrifice flies—where the batter intentionally makes an out in order to advance one or more baserunners
* Hits: times a base is reached safely, because of a batted, fair ball without a fielding error or [fielder's choice](/wiki/Fielder%27s_choice "Fielder's choice")
* Runs: times circling the bases and reaching home safely
* [Runs batted in](/wiki/Run_batted_in "Run batted in") (RBIs): number of runners who scored due to a batter's action (including the batter, in the case of a home run), except when batter grounded into double play or reached on an error
* Home runs: hits on which the batter successfully touched all four bases, without the contribution of a fielding error
* [Batting average](/wiki/Batting_average_(baseball) "Batting average (baseball)"): hits divided by at bats—the traditional measure of batting ability

The basic baserunning statistics include:[[155]](#cite_note-156)

* Stolen bases: times advancing to the next base entirely due to the runner's own efforts, generally while the pitcher is preparing to deliver or delivering the ball
* Caught stealing: times tagged out while attempting to steal a base

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Cy_young_pitching.jpg/250px-Cy_young_pitching.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cy_young_pitching.jpg)

[Cy Young](/wiki/Cy_Young "Cy Young")—the holder of many major league career marks, including wins and innings pitched, as well as losses—in 1908. MLB's annual [awards for the best pitcher in each league](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award") are named for Young.

The basic pitching statistics include:[[156]](#cite_note-157)

* [Wins](/wiki/Win%E2%80%93loss_record_(pitching) "Win–loss record (pitching)"): credited to pitcher on winning team who last pitched before the team took a lead that it never relinquished (a starting pitcher must pitch at least five innings to qualify for a win)
* [Losses](/wiki/Win%E2%80%93loss_record_(pitching) "Win–loss record (pitching)"): charged to pitcher on losing team who was pitching when the opposing team took a lead that it never relinquished
* [Saves](/wiki/Save_(baseball) "Save (baseball)"): games where the pitcher enters a game led by the pitcher's team, finishes the game without surrendering the lead, is not the winning pitcher, and either (a) the lead was three runs or less when the pitcher entered the game; (b) the potential tying run was on base, at bat, or [on deck](/wiki/On_deck_(baseball) "On deck (baseball)"); or (c) the pitcher pitched three or more innings
* [Innings pitched](/wiki/Innings_pitched "Innings pitched"): outs recorded while pitching divided by three (partial innings are conventionally recorded as, e.g., "5.2" or "7.1", the last digit actually representing thirds, not tenths, of an inning)
* Strikeouts: times pitching three strikes to a batter
* [Winning percentage](/wiki/Winning_percentage "Winning percentage"): wins divided by decisions (wins plus losses)
* [Earned run average](/wiki/Earned_run_average "Earned run average") (ERA): runs allowed, excluding those resulting from fielding errors, per nine innings pitched

The basic fielding statistics include:[[157]](#cite_note-158)

* [Putouts](/wiki/Putout "Putout"): times the fielder catches a fly ball, tags or forces out a runner, or otherwise directly effects an out
* [Assists](/wiki/Assist_(baseball) "Assist (baseball)"): times a putout by another fielder was recorded following the fielder touching the ball
* [Errors](/wiki/Error_(baseball) "Error (baseball)"): times the fielder fails to make a play that should have been made with common effort, and the batting team benefits as a result
* [Total chances](/wiki/Total_chances "Total chances"): putouts plus assists plus errors
* [Fielding average](/wiki/Fielding_average "Fielding average"): successful chances (putouts plus assists) divided by total chances

Among the many other statistics that are kept are those collectively known as *situational statistics*. For example, statistics can indicate which specific pitchers a certain batter performs best against. If a given situation statistically favors a certain batter, the manager of the fielding team may be more likely to change pitchers or have the pitcher [intentionally walk](/wiki/Intentional_base_on_balls "Intentional base on balls") the batter in order to face one who is less likely to succeed.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

### Sabermetrics

[Sabermetrics](/wiki/Sabermetrics "Sabermetrics") is the field of baseball statistical study and the development of new statistics and analytical tools. Such new statistics are also called sabermetrics. The term was coined around 1980 by one of the field's leading proponents, [Bill James](/wiki/Bill_James "Bill James"), and derives from the [Society for American Baseball Research](/wiki/Society_for_American_Baseball_Research "Society for American Baseball Research") (SABR).[[159]](#cite_note-160)

The growing popularity of sabermetrics since the early 1980s has brought more attention to two batting statistics that sabermetricians argue are much better gauges of a batter's skill than batting average:[[160]](#cite_note-161)

* [On-base percentage](/wiki/On-base_percentage "On-base percentage") (OBP) measures a batter's ability to get on base. It is calculated by taking the sum of the batter's successes in getting on base (hits plus walks plus hit by pitches) and dividing that by the batter's total plate appearances (at bats plus walks plus hit by pitches plus sacrifice flies), except for sacrifice bunts.[[161]](#cite_note-162)
* [Slugging percentage](/wiki/Slugging_percentage "Slugging percentage") (SLG) measures a batter's ability to hit for power. It is calculated by taking the batter's [total bases](/wiki/Total_bases "Total bases") (one per each single, two per double, three per triple, and four per home run) and dividing that by the batter's at bats.[[162]](#cite_note-163)

Some of the new statistics devised by sabermetricians have gained wide use:

* [On-base plus slugging](/wiki/On-base_plus_slugging "On-base plus slugging") (OPS) measures a batter's overall ability. It is calculated by adding the batter's on-base percentage and slugging percentage.[[163]](#cite_note-164)
* [Walks plus hits per inning pitched](/wiki/Walks_plus_hits_per_inning_pitched "Walks plus hits per inning pitched") (WHIP) measures a pitcher's ability at preventing hitters from reaching base. It is calculated by adding the number of walks and hits a pitcher surrendered, then dividing by the number of innings pitched.[[164]](#cite_note-165)
* [Wins Above Replacement](/wiki/Wins_Above_Replacement "Wins Above Replacement") (WAR) measures number of additional wins his team has achieved above the number of expected team wins if that player were substituted with a [replacement-level player](/wiki/Replacement-level_player "Replacement-level player").[[165]](#cite_note-166)

## Popularity and cultural impact

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Waseda_University_baseball_players.jpg/250px-Waseda_University_baseball_players.jpg)](/wiki/File:Waseda_University_baseball_players.jpg)

Two players on the baseball team of Tokyo, Japan's [Waseda University](/wiki/Waseda_University "Waseda University") in 1921

Writing in 1919, philosopher [Morris Raphael Cohen](/wiki/Morris_Raphael_Cohen "Morris Raphael Cohen") described baseball as the national religion of the US.[[166]](#cite_note-167) In the words of sports columnist [Jayson Stark](/wiki/Jayson_Stark "Jayson Stark"), baseball has long been "a unique paragon of American culture"—a status he sees as devastated by the steroid abuse scandal.[[167]](#cite_note-168) Baseball has an important place in other national cultures as well: Scholar Peter Bjarkman describes "how deeply the sport is ingrained in the history and culture of a nation such as Cuba, [and] how thoroughly it was radically reshaped and nativized in Japan."[[168]](#cite_note-169)

### Western Hemisphere
