American influence in the Western Hemisphere has meant that baseball grew significantly in the region.

#### In the United States

See also: [Baseball in Canada](/wiki/Baseball_in_Canada "Baseball in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/911_President_George_W._Bush_at_World_Series%2C_10302001..jpg/250px-911_President_George_W._Bush_at_World_Series%2C_10302001..jpg)](/wiki/File:911_President_George_W._Bush_at_World_Series,_10302001..jpg)

[President George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") taking advice before [throwing the first pitch](/wiki/2001_World_Series#September_11_and_the_month_of_November "2001 World Series") of World Series game 3, weeks after [9/11](/wiki/9/11 "9/11")

The major league game in the United States was originally targeted toward a middle-class, white-collar audience: relative to other spectator pastimes, the National League's set ticket price of 50 cents in 1876 was high, while the location of playing fields outside the inner city and the workweek daytime scheduling of games were also obstacles to a blue-collar audience.[[169]](#cite_note-170) A century later, the situation was very different. With the rise in popularity of other team sports with much higher average ticket prices—football, basketball, and hockey—professional baseball had become among the most popular blue-collar-oriented American spectator sports.[[170]](#cite_note-171)

Overall, baseball has a large following in the United States; a 2006 poll found that nearly half of Americans are fans.[[171]](#cite_note-172) This led to baseball being granted the title of "America's favorite pastime" by many American baseball fans.[[172]](#cite_note-173) The game was historically seen as contributing to the [melting pot](/wiki/Melting_pot "Melting pot") society of the nation, encouraging immigrants to integrate.[[173]](#cite_note-174) In the late 1900s and early 2000s, baseball's position compared to football in the United States moved in contradictory directions. In 2008, MLB set a revenue record of $6.5 billion, matching the NFL's revenue for the first time in decades.[[174]](#cite_note-175) A new MLB revenue record of more than $10 billion was set in 2017.[[175]](#cite_note-176) On the other hand, the percentage of American sports fans polled who named baseball as their favorite sport was 9%, compared to pro football at 37%.[[176]](#cite_note-177) In 1985, the respective figures were pro football 24%, baseball 23%.[[177]](#cite_note-178) Because there are so many more major league games played, there is no comparison in overall attendance.[[178]](#cite_note-179) In 2008, total attendance at major league games was the second-highest in history: 78.6 million, 0.7% off the record set the previous year.[[94]](#cite_note-MajorAtRec-95) The following year, amid the U.S. recession, attendance fell by 6.6% to 73.4 million.[[179]](#cite_note-Brown10-180) Eight years later, it dropped under 73 million.[[180]](#cite_note-181) Attendance at games held under the Minor League Baseball umbrella set a record in 2008, with 43.3 million.[[181]](#cite_note-182) While MLB games have not drawn the same national TV viewership as football games, MLB games are dominant in teams' local markets and regularly lead all programs in [primetime](/wiki/Primetime "Primetime") in their markets during the summer.[[182]](#cite_note-183) After multiple years of declines since 2017, baseball attendance has grown slightly in recent years. In 2024, attendance was 71.3 million, the highest since 2017.[[183]](#cite_note-184)

#### Latin America

See also: [Latin America–United States relations § Sports](/wiki/Latin_America%E2%80%93United_States_relations#Sports "Latin America–United States relations")

Baseball is very popular in Venezuela; in 2011, 95% of people surveyed claimed it to be the national sport.[[184]](#cite_note-185) In Nicaragua and Panama, baseball is also considered the national sport.[[185]](#cite_note-186)[[186]](#cite_note-187) While soccer is still more popular, baseball is a fast-growing sport [in Mexico](/wiki/Baseball_in_Mexico "Baseball in Mexico"). In 2024, top [Mexican League](/wiki/Mexican_Baseball_League "Mexican Baseball League") baseball teams outdraw some [Liga MX](/wiki/Liga_MX "Liga MX") teams in attendance.[[187]](#cite_note-188) Baseball also is somewhat popular in Colombia, though secondary to soccer, especially in coastal areas such as [Barranquilla](/wiki/Barranquilla "Barranquilla") and [Cartagena](/wiki/Cartagena,_Colombia "Cartagena, Colombia") which are [near the Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean_region_of_Colombia "Caribbean region of Colombia").[[188]](#cite_note-189) In recent years, baseball has grown in popularity in Colombia, especially in the capital of [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá"), due an increase of Venezuelan immigrants.[[189]](#cite_note-190) In Brazil, baseball fan popularity has grown in the last few years, thanks to MLB broadcasts in [Brazilian ESPN](/wiki/ESPN_(Brazil) "ESPN (Brazil)") and the historic silver medal in [2023 Pan-American games](/wiki/Baseball_at_the_2023_Pan_American_Games "Baseball at the 2023 Pan American Games"). However, it still lags behind [basketball](/wiki/Basketball_in_Brazil "Basketball in Brazil") and [American football](/wiki/American_football_in_Brazil "American football in Brazil") in the list of most played sports in Brazil.[[190]](#cite_note-191)[[191]](#cite_note-192) Baseball has also grown in popularity in Argentina and Peru in recent years due to Venezuelan immigrants.[[192]](#cite_note-193) Costa Rica has also seen a growth in the popularity of baseball. Since 1987, baseballs have exclusively been made in the Costa Rican town of [Turrialba](/wiki/Turrialba_(district) "Turrialba (district)").[[193]](#cite_note-194) Baseball is also played in [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras "Honduras"). In recent years, Major leaguer [Mauricio Dubón](/wiki/Mauricio_Dub%C3%B3n "Mauricio Dubón") has made efforts to increase support for the sport in the country.[[194]](#cite_note-195)

The sport's overall popularity in Latin America has assisted in [integrating Latin American migrants to the United States](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans#Sports "Hispanic and Latino Americans").[[195]](#cite_note-196)

#### Caribbean

Since the early 1980s, the Dominican Republic, in particular the city of [San Pedro de Macorís](/wiki/San_Pedro_de_Macor%C3%ADs "San Pedro de Macorís"), has been the major leagues' primary source of foreign talent.[[196]](#cite_note-197) In 2017, 83 of the 868 players on MLB Opening Day rosters (and disabled lists) were from the country. Among other Caribbean countries and territories, a combined 97 MLB players were born in Venezuela, Cuba, and Puerto Rico.[[197]](#cite_note-198) Hall-of-Famer [Roberto Clemente](/wiki/Roberto_Clemente "Roberto Clemente") remains one of the greatest national heroes in Puerto Rico's history.[[198]](#cite_note-199) While baseball has long been the island's primary athletic pastime, its once well-attended professional winter league has declined in popularity since 1990, when young Puerto Rican players began to be included in the major leagues' annual first-year player draft.[[199]](#cite_note-200) In Cuba, where baseball is by every reckoning the national sport,[[200]](#cite_note-201) the national team overshadows the city and provincial teams that play in the top-level domestic leagues.[[201]](#cite_note-WW-202) Baseball is also one of the most popular sports of [Aruba](/wiki/Aruba "Aruba") and [Curaçao](/wiki/Cura%C3%A7ao "Curaçao"),[[202]](#cite_note-203) and a growing sport in [The Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas") and the [US Virgin Islands](/wiki/US_Virgin_Islands "US Virgin Islands").[[203]](#cite_note-204)[[204]](#cite_note-205)

### Asia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Afghan_girl_playing_baseball_in_2002.jpg/250px-Afghan_girl_playing_baseball_in_2002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afghan_girl_playing_baseball_in_2002.jpg)

An Afghan girl playing baseball in August 2002

In East Asia, baseball is among the most popular sports in Japan,[[205]](#cite_note-206) Taiwan[[206]](#cite_note-207) and South Korea.[[207]](#cite_note-208) In Japan, where baseball is inarguably the [leading spectator team sport](/wiki/Baseball_in_Japan "Baseball in Japan"), combined revenue for the twelve teams in Nippon Professional Baseball (NPB), the body that oversees both the Central and Pacific Leagues, was estimated at $1 billion in 2007. Total NPB attendance for the year was approximately 20 million. While in the preceding two decades, MLB attendance grew by 50 percent and revenue nearly tripled, the comparable NPB figures were stagnant. There are concerns that MLB's growing interest in acquiring star Japanese players will hurt the game in their home country.[[208]](#cite_note-RW-209) Revenue figures are not released for the country's amateur system. Similarly, according to one official pronouncement, the sport's governing authority "has never taken into account attendance ... because its greatest interest has always been the development of athletes".[[209]](#cite_note-210) In Taiwan, baseball is one of the [most widely spectated sports, in TV and person](/wiki/Baseball_in_Taiwan "Baseball in Taiwan").[[210]](#cite_note-Taiwan_Culture_Portal-211)

Baseball has [grown significantly in China](/wiki/Baseball_in_China "Baseball in China") in recent years, with MLB estimating in 2019 that there are 21 million active fans in the country.[[211]](#cite_note-:0-212)

Baseball was considered a national pastime in the Philippines in the 1960s and 70s, but the sport has since declined in popularity. Since 2005, there have been efforts to revitalize the sport in the country.[[212]](#cite_note-renaissance-213)

Baseball saw a surge in popularity in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") following the countries' silver medal in baseball in the [2025 SEA Games](/wiki/2025_SEA_Games "2025 SEA Games"), which were hosted in Thailand.[[213]](#cite_note-214)

### Europe

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Tigers2017Champions.jpg/250px-Tigers2017Champions.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tigers2017Champions.jpg)

The [Tampere Tigers](/wiki/Tampere_Tigers "Tampere Tigers") celebrating the 2017 title in [Turku](/wiki/Turku "Turku"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")

Baseball is generally a [niche sport in Europe](/wiki/Baseball_in_Europe "Baseball in Europe").[[214]](#cite_note-215) However, baseball is somewhat popular [in the Netherlands](/wiki/Baseball_in_the_Netherlands "Baseball in the Netherlands"), where it is known as *honkbal*, thanks to influence from the United States after World War II and the Kingdoms control over Aruba and Curaçao where it is the most popular sport.[[215]](#cite_note-216) Baseball is also somewhat popular in portions of Italy, especially in the town of [Nettuno](/wiki/Nettuno "Nettuno"), due to influence from American soldiers after World War II.[[216]](#cite_note-217) Baseball has grown in recent years in the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), due in part to the Czech teams' success in the [2023 World Baseball Classic](/wiki/2023_World_Baseball_Classic "2023 World Baseball Classic").[[217]](#cite_note-218)

### Oceania

See also: [Baseball in New Zealand](/wiki/Baseball_in_New_Zealand "Baseball in New Zealand")

Baseball is one of the most popular sports in [Palau](/wiki/Palau "Palau") and [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam").[[218]](#cite_note-219)[[219]](#cite_note-220) In [Australia](/wiki/Baseball_in_Australia "Baseball in Australia"), baseball is a niche sport but the country does have a professional league. Because the league is played in winter for the western hemisphere, it attracts American and Korean professional players looking to play [winter league baseball](/wiki/Winter_league_baseball "Winter league baseball").[[220]](#cite_note-221) In 2026, there were more than 30 Australians playing [college baseball](/wiki/College_baseball "College baseball") in the United States compared to six in 2020.[[221]](#cite_note-222)

### Africa

The Dodgers operate a large baseball academy in [Mpigi](/wiki/Mpigi "Mpigi"), [Uganda](/wiki/Uganda "Uganda"), and there are also baseball programs in [Luwero](/wiki/Luwero "Luwero"), Uganda.[[222]](#cite_note-223) Baseball was popular in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") prior to World War 2 but popularity has since waned. There are still teams and attempts to raise the popularity of baseball in the country, including the introduction of [Baseball5](/wiki/Baseball5 "Baseball5").[[223]](#cite_note-224) There is a growing baseball program in [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"), which has resulted in professional contracts.[[224]](#cite_note-225) The Japan-Africa Baseball & Softball Foundation has created [Koshien high school baseball tournaments](/wiki/K%C5%8Dshien_baseball_tournaments "Kōshien baseball tournaments") in [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania"), [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana") and [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya"), which Tanzania being the most successful. There are further plans for similar tournaments in [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"), South Sudan, [Benin](/wiki/Benin "Benin"), [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") and [Zambia](/wiki/Zambia "Zambia").[[225]](#cite_note-226)

### Among children

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Baseball&action=edit), [Little League Baseball](/wiki/Little_League_Baseball "Little League Baseball") oversees leagues with 2 million participants in more than 80 countries.[[226]](#cite_note-227) The number of players has fallen since the 1990s, when 3 million children took part in Little League Baseball annually.[[227]](#cite_note-228) [Babe Ruth League](/wiki/Babe_Ruth_League "Babe Ruth League") teams have over 1 million participants.[[228]](#cite_note-229) According to the president of the [International Baseball Federation](/wiki/International_Baseball_Federation "International Baseball Federation") in 2009, between 300,000 and 500,000 women and girls play baseball around the world, including Little League and the introductory game of [Tee Ball](/wiki/Tee_Ball "Tee Ball").[[229]](#cite_note-230)

A varsity baseball team is an established part of [physical education](/wiki/Physical_education "Physical education") departments at most high schools and colleges in the United States.[[230]](#cite_note-MB-231) In 2015, nearly half a million high schoolers and over 34,000 collegians played on their schools' baseball teams.[[231]](#cite_note-232) By early in the 20th century, intercollegiate baseball was Japan's leading sport. Today, [high school baseball](/wiki/High_school_baseball_in_Japan "High school baseball in Japan") in particular is immensely popular there.[[232]](#cite_note-233) The final rounds of the two annual tournaments—the [National High School Baseball Invitational Tournament](/wiki/National_High_School_Baseball_Invitational_Tournament "National High School Baseball Invitational Tournament") in the spring, and the even more important [National High School Baseball Championship](/wiki/National_High_School_Baseball_Championship "National High School Baseball Championship") in the summer—are broadcast around the country. The tournaments are known, respectively, as Spring Koshien and Summer Koshien after the [55,000-capacity stadium](/wiki/Koshien_Stadium "Koshien Stadium") where they are played.[[233]](#cite_note-234) In Cuba, baseball is a mandatory part of the state system of physical education, which begins at age six. Talented children as young as seven are sent to special district schools for more intensive training—the first step on a ladder whose acme is the national baseball team.[[201]](#cite_note-WW-202)

### In popular culture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Honus_wagner_t206_baseball_card.jpg/250px-Honus_wagner_t206_baseball_card.jpg)](/wiki/File:Honus_wagner_t206_baseball_card.jpg)

The [American Tobacco Company](/wiki/American_Tobacco_Company "American Tobacco Company")'s line of baseball cards featured shortstop [Honus Wagner](/wiki/Honus_Wagner "Honus Wagner") of the [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Pirates "Pittsburgh Pirates") from 1909 to 1911. In 2007, [the card shown here](/wiki/T206_Honus_Wagner "T206 Honus Wagner") sold for $2.8 million.[[234]](#cite_note-235)

Baseball has had a broad impact on popular culture, both in the United States and elsewhere. Dozens of [English-language idioms have been derived from baseball](/wiki/English_language_idioms_derived_from_baseball "English language idioms derived from baseball"); in particular, the game is the source of a number of widely used [sexual euphemisms](/wiki/Baseball_metaphors_for_sex "Baseball metaphors for sex").[[235]](#cite_note-236) The first networked radio broadcasts in North America were of the [1922 World Series](/wiki/1922_World_Series "1922 World Series"): famed sportswriter [Grantland Rice](/wiki/Grantland_Rice "Grantland Rice") announced [play-by-play](/wiki/Play-by-play "Play-by-play") from New York City's [Polo Grounds](/wiki/Polo_Grounds "Polo Grounds") on [WJZ](/wiki/WABC_(AM)#WJZ_Newark_(1921–1923) "WABC (AM)")–[Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), which was connected by wire to [WGY](/wiki/WGY_(AM) "WGY (AM)")–[Schenectady, New York](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York"), and [WBZ](/wiki/WBZ_(AM) "WBZ (AM)")–[Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts").[[236]](#cite_note-237) The [baseball cap](/wiki/Baseball_cap "Baseball cap") has become a ubiquitous fashion item not only in the United States and Japan, but also in countries where the sport itself is not particularly popular, such as the United Kingdom.[[237]](#cite_note-238)

Baseball has inspired many works of art and entertainment. One of the first major examples, [Ernest Thayer](/wiki/Ernest_Thayer "Ernest Thayer")'s poem "[Casey at the Bat](/wiki/Casey_at_the_Bat "Casey at the Bat")", appeared in 1888; a wry description of the failure of a star player in what would now be called a "clutch situation", the poem became the source of [vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville") and other staged performances, audio recordings, film adaptations, and an opera, as well as a host of sequels and parodies in various media. There have been many [baseball movies](/wiki/Baseball_movie "Baseball movie"), including the [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards")–winning *[The Pride of the Yankees](/wiki/The_Pride_of_the_Yankees "The Pride of the Yankees")* (1942) and the Oscar nominees *[The Natural](/wiki/The_Natural_(film) "The Natural (film)")* (1984) and *[Field of Dreams](/wiki/Field_of_Dreams "Field of Dreams")* (1989). The [American Film Institute](/wiki/American_Film_Institute "American Film Institute")'s selection of the ten best sports movies includes *The Pride of the Yankees* at number 3 and *[Bull Durham](/wiki/Bull_Durham "Bull Durham")* (1988) at number 5.[[238]](#cite_note-239) Baseball has provided thematic material for hits on both stage—the [Adler](/wiki/Richard_Adler "Richard Adler")–[Ross](/wiki/Jerry_Ross_(composer) "Jerry Ross (composer)") musical *[Damn Yankees](/wiki/Damn_Yankees "Damn Yankees")*—and record—[George J. Gaskin](/wiki/George_J._Gaskin "George J. Gaskin")'s "Slide, Kelly, Slide", [Simon and Garfunkel](/wiki/Simon_and_Garfunkel "Simon and Garfunkel")'s "[Mrs. Robinson](/wiki/Mrs._Robinson "Mrs. Robinson")", and [John Fogerty](/wiki/John_Fogerty "John Fogerty")'s "[Centerfield](/wiki/Centerfield_(song) "Centerfield (song)")".[[239]](#cite_note-240) The baseball-inspired comedic sketch "[Who's on First?](/wiki/Who%27s_on_First%3F "Who's on First?")", popularized by [Abbott and Costello](/wiki/Abbott_and_Costello "Abbott and Costello") in 1938, quickly became famous; six decades later, *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* named it the best comedy routine of the 20th century.[[240]](#cite_note-241)

Literary works connected to the game include the short fiction of [Ring Lardner](/wiki/Ring_Lardner "Ring Lardner") and novels such as [Bernard Malamud](/wiki/Bernard_Malamud "Bernard Malamud")'s *[The Natural](/wiki/The_Natural "The Natural")* (the source for the movie), [Robert Coover](/wiki/Robert_Coover "Robert Coover")'s *[The Universal Baseball Association, Inc., J. Henry Waugh, Prop.](/wiki/The_Universal_Baseball_Association,_Inc.,_J._Henry_Waugh,_Prop. "The Universal Baseball Association, Inc., J. Henry Waugh, Prop.")*, [John Grisham](/wiki/John_Grisham "John Grisham")'s [Calico Joe](/wiki/Calico_Joe "Calico Joe") and [W. P. Kinsella](/wiki/W._P._Kinsella "W. P. Kinsella")'s *[Shoeless Joe](/wiki/Shoeless_Joe_(novel) "Shoeless Joe (novel)")* (the source for *Field of Dreams*). Baseball's literary canon also includes the beat reportage of [Damon Runyon](/wiki/Damon_Runyon "Damon Runyon"); the columns of Grantland Rice, [Red Smith](/wiki/Red_Smith_(sportswriter) "Red Smith (sportswriter)"), [Dick Young](/wiki/Dick_Young_(sportswriter) "Dick Young (sportswriter)"), and [Peter Gammons](/wiki/Peter_Gammons "Peter Gammons"); and the essays of [Roger Angell](/wiki/Roger_Angell "Roger Angell"). Among the celebrated nonfiction books in the field are [Lawrence S. Ritter](/wiki/Lawrence_S._Ritter "Lawrence S. Ritter")'s *[The Glory of Their Times](/wiki/The_Glory_of_Their_Times "The Glory of Their Times")*, [Roger Kahn](/wiki/Roger_Kahn "Roger Kahn")'s *[The Boys of Summer](/wiki/The_Boys_of_Summer_(book) "The Boys of Summer (book)")*, and [Michael Lewis](/wiki/Michael_Lewis_(author) "Michael Lewis (author)")'s *[Moneyball](/wiki/Moneyball:_The_Art_of_Winning_an_Unfair_Game "Moneyball: The Art of Winning an Unfair Game")*. The 1970 publication of major league pitcher [Jim Bouton](/wiki/Jim_Bouton "Jim Bouton")'s tell-all chronicle *[Ball Four](/wiki/Ball_Four "Ball Four")* is considered a turning point in the reporting of professional sports.[[241]](#cite_note-242)

Baseball has also inspired the creation of new cultural forms. [Baseball cards](/wiki/Baseball_card "Baseball card") were introduced in the late 19th century as [trade cards](/wiki/Trade_card "Trade card"). A typical example featured an image of a baseball player on one side and advertising for a business on the other. In the early 1900s they were produced widely as promotional items by tobacco and confectionery companies. The 1930s saw the popularization of the modern style of baseball card, with a player photograph accompanied on the rear by statistics and biographical data. Baseball cards—many of which are now prized collectibles—are the source of the much broader [trading card](/wiki/Trading_card "Trading card") industry, involving similar products for different sports and non-sports-related fields.[[242]](#cite_note-243)

Modern [fantasy sports](/wiki/Fantasy_sports "Fantasy sports") began in 1980 with the invention of [Rotisserie League Baseball](/wiki/Fantasy_baseball#Rotisserie_League_Baseball "Fantasy baseball") by New York writer [Daniel Okrent](/wiki/Daniel_Okrent "Daniel Okrent") and several friends. Participants in a Rotisserie league draft notional teams from the list of active MLB players and play out an entire imaginary season with game outcomes based on the players' latest real-world statistics. Rotisserie-style play quickly became a phenomenon. Now known more generically as [fantasy baseball](/wiki/Fantasy_baseball "Fantasy baseball"), it has inspired similar games based on an array of different sports.[[243]](#cite_note-244) The field boomed with increasing Internet access and new fantasy sports-related websites. By 2008, 29.9 million people in the United States and Canada were playing fantasy sports, spending $800 million on the hobby.[[244]](#cite_note-245) The burgeoning popularity of fantasy baseball is also credited with the increasing attention paid to sabermetrics—first among fans, only later among baseball professionals.[[245]](#cite_note-246)

### Derivative games

Main article: [Variations of baseball](/wiki/Variations_of_baseball "Variations of baseball")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Queens_stickball.jpg/250px-Queens_stickball.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queens_stickball.jpg)

[Stickball](/wiki/Stickball "Stickball") is a common street variant of baseball which often features impromptu adaptations. (Note the painted strike zone on the wall behind the batter.)

Informal [variations of baseball](/wiki/Variations_of_baseball "Variations of baseball") have popped up over time, with games like [corkball](/wiki/Corkball "Corkball") reflecting local traditions and allowing the game to be played in diverse environments.[[246]](#cite_note-247) Two variations of baseball, [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball") and [Baseball5](/wiki/Baseball5 "Baseball5"), are internationally governed alongside baseball by the [World Baseball Softball Confederation](/wiki/World_Baseball_Softball_Confederation "World Baseball Softball Confederation").[[247]](#cite_note-248)

#### British baseball

Main article: [British baseball](/wiki/British_baseball "British baseball")

American [professional baseball](/wiki/Professional_baseball "Professional baseball") teams toured Britain in 1874 and 1889, and had a great effect on similar sports in Britain. In Wales and Merseyside, a strong community game had already developed with skills and plays more in keeping with the American game and the Welsh began to informally adopt the name "baseball" (Pêl Fas), to reflect the American style. By the 1890s, calls were made to follow the success of other working class sports (like Rugby in Wales and Soccer in Merseyside) and adopt a distinct set of rules and bureaucracy.[[248]](#cite_note-249) During the 1892 season rules for the game of "baseball" were agreed and the game was officially codified.[[249]](#cite_note-250)

#### Finnish baseball

Main article: [Pesäpallo](/wiki/Pes%C3%A4pallo "Pesäpallo")

Finnish baseball, also known as pesäpallo, is a combination of traditional ball-batting team games and North American baseball, invented by [Lauri "Tahko" Pihkala](/wiki/Lauri_Pihkala "Lauri Pihkala") in the 1920s.[[250]](#cite_note-INTRO2-251)[[251]](#cite_note-252) The basic idea of pesäpallo is similar to that of baseball: the offense tries to score by hitting the ball successfully and running through the bases, while the defense tries to put the batter and runners out. One of the most important differences between pesäpallo and baseball is that the ball is pitched vertically, which makes hitting the ball, as well as controlling the power and direction of the hit, much easier. This gives the offensive game more variety, speed, and tactical aspects compared to baseball.[[250]](#cite_note-INTRO2-251)

Every detail of play is recorded, and a man's mistakes are tabulated as well as his successes... A line in a cricket score may read, 'Lockwood, *caught* Stoddart, *bowled* J. T. Hearne; 30.'... [T]here is so much that is left out! There is no mention of the fact that O'Brien missed Lockwood before he had scored, and that somebody else failed to take a chance when his score was ten. These are items that go to make cricket history; but there is no record of them in the analysis... The man who catches a ball is thought worthy of mention, but the man who muffs one does not suffer by publicity."
